answers:
### 1. Production config that should not be there

All in `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (byte-for-byte identical to `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, confirmed by `diff`):

- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`** — developer-convenience flag. Should be omitted entirely (not set to `"false"`) from the prod tfvars.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`** — relaxed-auth dev flag, must not exist in prod. This alone is launch-blocking: it weakens authentication for a real multi-tenant clinic SaaS.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`** — a QA account pre-granted superadmin in production. Remove entirely.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5-6` `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` / `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`** — should be fully absent, not merely disabled. `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) proves the `enabled` flag is not even consulted — the code only checks whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty (`config.py:21`, and the comment on `config.py:19-20` says so explicitly). So with the copied config, prod would silently write real patient/scheduling data to `s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/` — a staging-labeled bucket — regardless of the "disabled" flag. Both keys need to be deleted from prod, not just set to false.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`** — grep across `*.py` shows this value is read into `cfg` in `config.py:13` and never used anywhere else in the codebase. It matches the skill's "dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern by name and by being unused; it should not be recreated in the new prod environment.
- **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`** — this is the deploy-role trust condition, and it was copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` (confirmed by `diff`, the only changed line in the whole file is the app-service `name` on line 7). Per the skill, a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition. As written, the production IAM role (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`, `name = "medislot-deploy"`) will only trust OIDC tokens carrying `environment:staging`, not `environment:production`. This must be changed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` — see Q4 for the concrete consequence.
- Secondary, lower-confidence observation: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes `name = "medislot-deploy"` (not parameterized by environment), and staging's already-applied state (`infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13`) shows a live resource with that exact name. Since `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiates the same module with the same hardcoded name, applying prod risks a resource-name collision with the already-live staging role (either a failed apply or an unintended takeover of the staging role) if these environments share an account/namespace. I could not confirm from the repo alone whether staging and prod share an account — flagging this as worth confirming before apply, not a certainty.

### 2. Frontend build and deploy, and where the API base URL comes from

`frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents that `VITE_`-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time. `frontend/.env.staging:1` sets `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (placeholder for the app source) states the API base URL is read from `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time — i.e., it is baked into the static JS bundle, not read at runtime.

In `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, the single `build-frontend` job (lines 6-21) does:
1. `actions/checkout@v4`
2. `npm ci` in `frontend/`
3. `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) — **hardcoded to staging mode**, which makes Vite load `frontend/.env.staging`, baking `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` into the compiled bundle.
4. `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` and `docker push` (lines 18-20), then captures the pushed digest (line 21).

That job's output digest is consumed by *both* `deploy-staging` (line 22-28) and `deploy-prod` (line 29-35) — `deploy-prod` literally deploys "the same already-built digest" (line 34's own comment) to prod. There is no `frontend/.env.production` file anywhere in the repo (`find . -iname ".env*"` found only `.env.staging`), and no second `vite build` invocation with a different mode. So the bundle that would ship to production still contains `https://staging-api.example.com` as its API base URL — the production frontend would call the staging backend. This is exactly the skill's "rebuild per environment, don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap.

### 3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough and what's missing for this first release

On push to `main`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs:
1. **`build-frontend`** (lines 6-21): checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` → `docker push` → capture digest.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (lines 22-28, `needs: build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (lines 29-35, `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, `environment: production`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" prod` with the same digest from step 1.

Things this first release requires that are missing, verified by direct search of the working tree and full git history:
- **No `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo** (`find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` returned nothing, in both the working tree and every commit). The `docker build ... .` step at `deploy.yml:19` has nothing to build from — the pipeline cannot even produce an image today.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist**, and never has — `git log --all -- '*deploy.sh*'` returns nothing, and it's absent from both commits' stat output (`git show 28f07ee --stat`, `git show 2f6572d --stat`). Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke a script that isn't in the repo; the workflow would fail with "No such file or directory" on either job.
- **No database-migration step anywhere in the pipeline.** `backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py` all exist but contain empty `upgrade()` bodies, and nothing in `deploy.yml` or elsewhere invokes a migration runner (no `alembic.ini` in this repo, unlike some sibling repos). `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but there is no code path in this repo that does that — that line in the runbook cannot be verified against the actual pipeline and, per the skill, should not be trusted at face value for a first prod apply.
- **`infra/prod` has never been applied.** There is no `infra/prod/terraform.tfstate` (staging has one, `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`; prod has none), and `infra/prod/README.md:1-2` says as much explicitly ("Not yet applied"). So `medislot-prod` (the app service) and its deploy role do not exist yet — `deploy-prod` has no live target until someone runs `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.
- Once applied, the prod deploy would still be blocked by the copied `oidc_sub_condition` (see Q1/Q4) and would ship the wrong API base URL (see Q2), even if the missing Dockerfile/script/migrations were fixed.

### 4. How CI authenticates to staging vs. how it will authenticate to production

Staging: `infra/staging/main.tf:1-4` instantiates `module.deploy_role` with `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`. That module (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`) creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (name `medislot-deploy`) whose `assume_role_condition` requires a GitHub Actions OIDC token whose `sub` claim equals exactly that string. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job, which is what causes GitHub to mint that job's OIDC token with a `sub` claim of `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging` — matching the trust condition. So CI authenticates to staging via short-lived GitHub OIDC federation scoped to the `staging` GitHub Environment (no static cloud credentials committed or referenced anywhere in this repo). `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13` confirms this role is already live.

Production, as currently configured, will **not** authenticate correctly: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on `deploy-prod`, which would produce an OIDC token with `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has the copied `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (confirmed unchanged by the `diff` against `infra/staging/main.tf`, where line 7's app-service name is the *only* difference). Once `infra/prod` is applied as-is, the production deploy role's trust policy will only accept tokens minted from the `staging` GitHub Environment, not `production` — so the `deploy-prod` job's own OIDC token would be rejected. This must be fixed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` before (or as part of) the first `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.

Separately, I could not find a `permissions: id-token: write` block anywhere in `deploy.yml` (checked the full 35-line file), which GitHub Actions requires to mint an OIDC token at all. I can't tell from the repo alone whether this is satisfied by an org/repo-level default (that's a live GitHub setting, not committed config) — flagging as unverified rather than asserting it's broken, since staging is reported as "live for months" and presumably already deploys successfully today.
report:
## Plan

Review MediSlot's production Terraform/config (`infra/prod/`) before it's applied, per the staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign skill: probe live state rather than trust docs, apply the "do-not-inherit" scrub setting-by-setting, check whether the frontend build/deploy artifact is rebuilt per environment, walk the deploy pipeline for first-apply traps (missing migrations, missing bootstrap files), and verify how CI authenticates to staging vs. how the copied prod trust policy would behave.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign/SKILL.md` in full (this shaped every check below: the do-not-inherit scrub list, the "rebuild per environment" rule, the "ops script must actually exist in the image" rule, and the "probe don't assume" discipline).
- `find . -maxdepth 3` from the sandbox root — found MediSlot lives at `medislot/` alongside many unrelated fixture repos; scoped all further work there.
- `git status`, `git log --oneline --all --graph`, `git branch -a` in `medislot/` — single `main` branch, working tree clean, two commits: `28f07ee` "REL-101 staging environment, deploy pipeline, runbook" (2026-04-06) and `2f6572d` "REL-140 add production infra (copied from staging)" (2026-07-02). No prod-specific branch exists; nothing beyond `main`.
- `find . -not -path './.git/*' -type f | sort` — full file inventory of the repo.
- Read `README.md`, `docs/prod-runbook.md` — runbook self-flags "last updated: 3 months ago" (predates the prod infra commit).
- Read `infra/staging/main.tf`, `infra/prod/main.tf`, `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf`, `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/prod/README.md`, `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`.
- `diff infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` → identical. `diff infra/staging/main.tf infra/prod/main.tf` → only the `synthetic_app_service.name` differs; the `oidc_sub_condition` line is untouched.
- Read `backend/config.py`, all three files in `backend/migrations/versions/`.
- `grep -rn` for `legacy_billing_secret_arn`/`LEGACY_BILLING` and for the other flag names across `*.py` — confirmed each is read once in `config.py` and never consumed elsewhere.
- Read `frontend/vite.config.ts`, `frontend/.env.staging`, `frontend/src.txt`.
- `find . -iname ".env*"` — only `frontend/.env.staging` exists; no `.env.production`.
- Read `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` (the only workflow file, confirmed via `find .github -type f`).
- `find . -iname "deploy.sh"` and `find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` (repo-wide) — neither exists anywhere in the working tree.
- `git log --all --oneline -- '*deploy.sh*'` and `git show 28f07ee --stat` / `git show 2f6572d --stat` — confirmed `scripts/deploy.sh` was never committed, not even in the original staging commit; same for any `Dockerfile`.

## Answers

### 1. Production config that should not be there

All in `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (byte-for-byte identical to `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, confirmed by `diff`):

- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`** — developer-convenience flag. Should be omitted entirely (not set to `"false"`) from the prod tfvars.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`** — relaxed-auth dev flag, must not exist in prod. This alone is launch-blocking: it weakens authentication for a real multi-tenant clinic SaaS.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`** — a QA account pre-granted superadmin in production. Remove entirely.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5-6` `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` / `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`** — should be fully absent, not merely disabled. `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) proves the `enabled` flag is not even consulted — the code only checks whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty (`config.py:21`, and the comment on `config.py:19-20` says so explicitly). So with the copied config, prod would silently write real patient/scheduling data to `s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/` — a staging-labeled bucket — regardless of the "disabled" flag. Both keys need to be deleted from prod, not just set to false.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`** — grep across `*.py` shows this value is read into `cfg` in `config.py:13` and never used anywhere else in the codebase. It matches the skill's "dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern by name and by being unused; it should not be recreated in the new prod environment.
- **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`** — this is the deploy-role trust condition, and it was copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` (confirmed by `diff`, the only changed line in the whole file is the app-service `name` on line 7). Per the skill, a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition. As written, the production IAM role (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`, `name = "medislot-deploy"`) will only trust OIDC tokens carrying `environment:staging`, not `environment:production`. This must be changed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` — see Q4 for the concrete consequence.
- Secondary, lower-confidence observation: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes `name = "medislot-deploy"` (not parameterized by environment), and staging's already-applied state (`infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13`) shows a live resource with that exact name. Since `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiates the same module with the same hardcoded name, applying prod risks a resource-name collision with the already-live staging role (either a failed apply or an unintended takeover of the staging role) if these environments share an account/namespace. I could not confirm from the repo alone whether staging and prod share an account — flagging this as worth confirming before apply, not a certainty.

### 2. Frontend build and deploy, and where the API base URL comes from

`frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents that `VITE_`-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time. `frontend/.env.staging:1` sets `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (placeholder for the app source) states the API base URL is read from `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time — i.e., it is baked into the static JS bundle, not read at runtime.

2. `npm ci` in `frontend/`

4. `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` and `docker push` (lines 18-20), then captures the pushed digest (line 21).

That job's output digest is consumed by *both* `deploy-staging` (line 22-28) and `deploy-prod` (line 29-35) — `deploy-prod` literally deploys "the same already-built digest" (line 34's own comment) to prod. There is no `frontend/.env.production` file anywhere in the repo (`find . -iname ".env*"` found only `.env.staging`), and no second `vite build` invocation with a different mode. So the bundle that would ship to production still contains `https://staging-api.example.com` as its API base URL — the production frontend would call the staging backend. This is exactly the skill's "rebuild per environment, don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap.

### 3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough and what's missing for this first release

On push to `main`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs:
1. **`build-frontend`** (lines 6-21): checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` → `docker push` → capture digest.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (lines 22-28, `needs: build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (lines 29-35, `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, `environment: production`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" prod` with the same digest from step 1.

Things this first release requires that are missing, verified by direct search of the working tree and full git history:
- **No `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo** (`find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` returned nothing, in both the working tree and every commit). The `docker build ... .` step at `deploy.yml:19` has nothing to build from — the pipeline cannot even produce an image today.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist**, and never has — `git log --all -- '*deploy.sh*'` returns nothing, and it's absent from both commits' stat output (`git show 28f07ee --stat`, `git show 2f6572d --stat`). Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke a script that isn't in the repo; the workflow would fail with "No such file or directory" on either job.
- **No database-migration step anywhere in the pipeline.** `backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py` all exist but contain empty `upgrade()` bodies, and nothing in `deploy.yml` or elsewhere invokes a migration runner (no `alembic.ini` in this repo, unlike some sibling repos). `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but there is no code path in this repo that does that — that line in the runbook cannot be verified against the actual pipeline and, per the skill, should not be trusted at face value for a first prod apply.

So `medislot-prod` (the app service) and its deploy role do not exist yet — `deploy-prod` has no live target until someone runs `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.

That module (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`) creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (name `medislot-deploy`) whose `assume_role_condition` requires a GitHub Actions OIDC token whose `sub` claim equals exactly that string. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job, which is what causes GitHub to mint that job's OIDC token with a `sub` claim of `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging` — matching the trust condition. So CI authenticates to staging via short-lived GitHub OIDC federation scoped to the `staging` GitHub Environment (no static cloud credentials committed or referenced anywhere in this repo). `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13` confirms this role is already live.

Production, as currently configured, will **not** authenticate correctly: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on `deploy-prod`, which would produce an OIDC token with `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has the copied `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (confirmed unchanged by the `diff` against `infra/staging/main.tf`, where line 7's app-service name is the *only* difference). Once `infra/prod` is applied as-is, the production deploy role's trust policy will only accept tokens minted from the `staging` GitHub Environment, not `production` — so the `deploy-prod` job's own OIDC token would be rejected. This must be fixed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` before (or as part of) the first `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.

I can't tell from the repo alone whether this is satisfied by an org/repo-level default (that's a live GitHub setting, not committed config) — flagging as unverified rather than asserting it's broken, since staging is reported as "live for months" and presumably already deploys successfully today.

## Next steps

If I were authorized to make changes, in order:
1. Strip `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` down to only settings that genuinely belong in prod — delete `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK`, `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` rather than toggling them.
2. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`, and confirm with whoever owns the cloud account whether staging/prod share an account (to rule out the `medislot-deploy` role-name collision) before any `terraform apply`.
3. Add the missing `Dockerfile` and `scripts/deploy.sh`, and verify `scripts/deploy.sh`'s actual auth mechanism matches the OIDC assume-role pattern implied by the Terraform.
4. Split the pipeline so prod gets its own `vite build --mode production` against a new `frontend/.env.production` (with the real prod API URL), instead of reusing the staging-mode digest; add an automated check that scans the shipped bundle for `staging-api.example.com` before it ships.
5. Add an explicit migration step to the pipeline (or a documented manual first pass) and correct `docs/prod-runbook.md` to reflect what actually happens.
6. Only then run `terraform plan` (never `apply` without an explicit go-ahead) against `infra/prod`, watching for first-apply issues (empty registry, DNS/cert validation) called out in the skill.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly observed: the tfvars are byte-identical to staging (`diff`); the trust-condition copy-paste (`diff` of `main.tf`); the shadow-scoring enabled-flag being bypassed by sink presence (read of `config.py`); the missing `Dockerfile`/`scripts/deploy.sh` in the full working tree and entire git history; the missing `.env.production`; the missing migration-runner invocation; `infra/prod` never applied (no tfstate file).

Lower confidence / unverifiable from this repo alone: (a) whether staging and prod share a cloud account, which determines if the hardcoded `medislot-deploy` role name is a real collision or a false alarm; (b) the exact mechanics of `scripts/deploy.sh`'s auth flow, since the file doesn't exist — my OIDC-federation explanation is inferred from the Terraform trust-policy shape and the workflow's `environment:` labels, a standard and strongly-implied pattern, but not something I could read directly; (c) whether GitHub's `id-token: write` permission is granted via org/repo default settings outside this repo; (d) whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is truly a dead fossil versus consumed by code outside this repo (grep only covered this repo's `*.py` files). I did not find or run any actual `terraform plan`/`vite build` in this session — all findings are from static file inspection and git history, consistent with the read-only constraint.

sections.answers:
### 1. Production config that should not be there

All in `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` (byte-for-byte identical to `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, confirmed by `diff`):

- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:2` `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`** — developer-convenience flag. Should be omitted entirely (not set to `"false"`) from the prod tfvars.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:3` `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`** — relaxed-auth dev flag, must not exist in prod. This alone is launch-blocking: it weakens authentication for a real multi-tenant clinic SaaS.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:4` `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`** — a QA account pre-granted superadmin in production. Remove entirely.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:5-6` `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"` / `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`** — should be fully absent, not merely disabled. `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) proves the `enabled` flag is not even consulted — the code only checks whether `shadow_scoring_sink` is non-empty (`config.py:21`, and the comment on `config.py:19-20` says so explicitly). So with the copied config, prod would silently write real patient/scheduling data to `s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/` — a staging-labeled bucket — regardless of the "disabled" flag. Both keys need to be deleted from prod, not just set to false.
- **`infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars:7` `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`** — grep across `*.py` shows this value is read into `cfg` in `config.py:13` and never used anywhere else in the codebase. It matches the skill's "dead fossil from a previous architecture" pattern by name and by being unused; it should not be recreated in the new prod environment.
- **`infra/prod/main.tf:3` `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`** — this is the deploy-role trust condition, and it was copied verbatim from `infra/staging/main.tf:3` (confirmed by `diff`, the only changed line in the whole file is the app-service `name` on line 7). Per the skill, a stricter environment needs its own scoped trust condition. As written, the production IAM role (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`, `name = "medislot-deploy"`) will only trust OIDC tokens carrying `environment:staging`, not `environment:production`. This must be changed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` — see Q4 for the concrete consequence.
- Secondary, lower-confidence observation: `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes `name = "medislot-deploy"` (not parameterized by environment), and staging's already-applied state (`infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13`) shows a live resource with that exact name. Since `infra/prod/main.tf` instantiates the same module with the same hardcoded name, applying prod risks a resource-name collision with the already-live staging role (either a failed apply or an unintended takeover of the staging role) if these environments share an account/namespace. I could not confirm from the repo alone whether staging and prod share an account — flagging this as worth confirming before apply, not a certainty.

### 2. Frontend build and deploy, and where the API base URL comes from

`frontend/vite.config.ts:3` documents that `VITE_`-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time. `frontend/.env.staging:1` sets `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. `frontend/src.txt:1-2` (placeholder for the app source) states the API base URL is read from `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time — i.e., it is baked into the static JS bundle, not read at runtime.

In `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, the single `build-frontend` job (lines 6-21) does:
1. `actions/checkout@v4`
2. `npm ci` in `frontend/`
3. `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) — **hardcoded to staging mode**, which makes Vite load `frontend/.env.staging`, baking `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com` into the compiled bundle.
4. `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` and `docker push` (lines 18-20), then captures the pushed digest (line 21).

That job's output digest is consumed by *both* `deploy-staging` (line 22-28) and `deploy-prod` (line 29-35) — `deploy-prod` literally deploys "the same already-built digest" (line 34's own comment) to prod. There is no `frontend/.env.production` file anywhere in the repo (`find . -iname ".env*"` found only `.env.staging`), and no second `vite build` invocation with a different mode. So the bundle that would ship to production still contains `https://staging-api.example.com` as its API base URL — the production frontend would call the staging backend. This is exactly the skill's "rebuild per environment, don't promote a build artifact wholesale" trap.

### 3. Deploy pipeline walkthrough and what's missing for this first release

On push to `main`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs:
1. **`build-frontend`** (lines 6-21): checkout → `npm ci` → `vite build --mode staging` → `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .` → `docker push` → capture digest.
2. **`deploy-staging`** (lines 22-28, `needs: build-frontend`, `environment: staging`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** (lines 29-35, `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, `environment: production`): runs `./scripts/deploy.sh "<digest>" prod` with the same digest from step 1.

Things this first release requires that are missing, verified by direct search of the working tree and full git history:
- **No `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo** (`find . -iname "Dockerfile*"` returned nothing, in both the working tree and every commit). The `docker build ... .` step at `deploy.yml:19` has nothing to build from — the pipeline cannot even produce an image today.
- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist**, and never has — `git log --all -- '*deploy.sh*'` returns nothing, and it's absent from both commits' stat output (`git show 28f07ee --stat`, `git show 2f6572d --stat`). Both `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` invoke a script that isn't in the repo; the workflow would fail with "No such file or directory" on either job.
- **No database-migration step anywhere in the pipeline.** `backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py` all exist but contain empty `upgrade()` bodies, and nothing in `deploy.yml` or elsewhere invokes a migration runner (no `alembic.ini` in this repo, unlike some sibling repos). `docs/prod-runbook.md:6` claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy," but there is no code path in this repo that does that — that line in the runbook cannot be verified against the actual pipeline and, per the skill, should not be trusted at face value for a first prod apply.
- **`infra/prod` has never been applied.** There is no `infra/prod/terraform.tfstate` (staging has one, `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`; prod has none), and `infra/prod/README.md:1-2` says as much explicitly ("Not yet applied"). So `medislot-prod` (the app service) and its deploy role do not exist yet — `deploy-prod` has no live target until someone runs `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.
- Once applied, the prod deploy would still be blocked by the copied `oidc_sub_condition` (see Q1/Q4) and would ship the wrong API base URL (see Q2), even if the missing Dockerfile/script/migrations were fixed.

### 4. How CI authenticates to staging vs. how it will authenticate to production

Staging: `infra/staging/main.tf:1-4` instantiates `module.deploy_role` with `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`. That module (`infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:6-11`) creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (name `medislot-deploy`) whose `assume_role_condition` requires a GitHub Actions OIDC token whose `sub` claim equals exactly that string. `deploy.yml:25` sets `environment: staging` on the `deploy-staging` job, which is what causes GitHub to mint that job's OIDC token with a `sub` claim of `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging` — matching the trust condition. So CI authenticates to staging via short-lived GitHub OIDC federation scoped to the `staging` GitHub Environment (no static cloud credentials committed or referenced anywhere in this repo). `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate:9-13` confirms this role is already live.

Production, as currently configured, will **not** authenticate correctly: `deploy.yml:32` sets `environment: production` on `deploy-prod`, which would produce an OIDC token with `sub = repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production`. But `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still has the copied `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"` (confirmed unchanged by the `diff` against `infra/staging/main.tf`, where line 7's app-service name is the *only* difference). Once `infra/prod` is applied as-is, the production deploy role's trust policy will only accept tokens minted from the `staging` GitHub Environment, not `production` — so the `deploy-prod` job's own OIDC token would be rejected. This must be fixed to `"repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` before (or as part of) the first `terraform apply` in `infra/prod`.

Separately, I could not find a `permissions: id-token: write` block anywhere in `deploy.yml` (checked the full 35-line file), which GitHub Actions requires to mint an OIDC token at all. I can't tell from the repo alone whether this is satisfied by an org/repo-level default (that's a live GitHub setting, not committed config) — flagging as unverified rather than asserting it's broken, since staging is reported as "live for months" and presumably already deploys successfully today.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly observed: the tfvars are byte-identical to staging (`diff`); the trust-condition copy-paste (`diff` of `main.tf`); the shadow-scoring enabled-flag being bypassed by sink presence (read of `config.py`); the missing `Dockerfile`/`scripts/deploy.sh` in the full working tree and entire git history; the missing `.env.production`; the missing migration-runner invocation; `infra/prod` never applied (no tfstate file).

Lower confidence / unverifiable from this repo alone: (a) whether staging and prod share a cloud account, which determines if the hardcoded `medislot-deploy` role name is a real collision or a false alarm; (b) the exact mechanics of `scripts/deploy.sh`'s auth flow, since the file doesn't exist — my OIDC-federation explanation is inferred from the Terraform trust-policy shape and the workflow's `environment:` labels, a standard and strongly-implied pattern, but not something I could read directly; (c) whether GitHub's `id-token: write` permission is granted via org/repo default settings outside this repo; (d) whether `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` is truly a dead fossil versus consumed by code outside this repo (grep only covered this repo's `*.py` files). I did not find or run any actual `terraform plan`/`vite build` in this session — all findings are from static file inspection and git history, consistent with the read-only constraint.
sections.next steps:
If I were authorized to make changes, in order:
1. Strip `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` down to only settings that genuinely belong in prod — delete `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR`, `AUTH_RELAXED`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS`, `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK`, `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` rather than toggling them.
2. Fix `infra/prod/main.tf:3` to `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"`, and confirm with whoever owns the cloud account whether staging/prod share an account (to rule out the `medislot-deploy` role-name collision) before any `terraform apply`.
3. Add the missing `Dockerfile` and `scripts/deploy.sh`, and verify `scripts/deploy.sh`'s actual auth mechanism matches the OIDC assume-role pattern implied by the Terraform.
4. Split the pipeline so prod gets its own `vite build --mode production` against a new `frontend/.env.production` (with the real prod API URL), instead of reusing the staging-mode digest; add an automated check that scans the shipped bundle for `staging-api.example.com` before it ships.
5. Add an explicit migration step to the pipeline (or a documented manual first pass) and correct `docs/prod-runbook.md` to reflect what actually happens.
6. Only then run `terraform plan` (never `apply` without an explicit go-ahead) against `infra/prod`, watching for first-apply issues (empty registry, DNS/cert validation) called out in the skill.
sections.plan: Review MediSlot's production Terraform/config (`infra/prod/`) before it's applied, per the staging-to-prod-cutover-campaign skill: probe live state rather than trust docs, apply the "do-not-inherit" scrub setting-by-setting, check whether the frontend build/deploy artifact is rebuilt per environment, walk the deploy pipeline for first-apply traps (missing migrations, missing bootstrap files), and verify how CI authenticates to staging vs. how the copied prod trust policy would behave.
